content.text: In real-world situations, data sets are often so large that analyzing ungrouped data becomes impractical. For example, analyzing the heights of 1000 students individually would be cumbersome. Instead, data is organized into class intervals (groups), creating grouped data. This chapter extends the concepts of mean, median, and mode from ungrouped to grouped data.

Measures of central tendency (mean, median, and mode) help us understand the typical or central value in a dataset. For grouped data, we use specialized methods that account for the loss of information inherent in grouping while maintaining computational efficiency.

When data is grouped into class intervals, we assume that all observations in a class are concentrated at the class mark (or class midpoint). The class mark is found by averaging the upper and lower limits of the class interval.

content.title: Class Mark Formula
content.text: Class mark (xi) = (Lower class limit + Upper class limit) / 2. For example, for the class interval 10-25, the class mark = (10 + 25) / 2 = 17.5.

The direct method calculates the mean by finding the sum of the products of class marks and their frequencies, then dividing by the total frequency. This method is straightforward but can involve large calculations.

content.title: Direct Method Formula
content.text: Mean (x̄) = Σ(fi × xi) / Σfi, where fi is the frequency of class i and xi is the class mark of class i.

content.title: Example 1: Direct Method
content.problem: Find the mean of the frequency distribution: Class 10-20 (frequency 5), 20-30 (frequency 10), 30-40 (frequency 8), 40-50 (frequency 2).

content.text: When class marks are large numbers, the direct method involves tedious calculations. The assumed mean method simplifies this by choosing an arbitrary class mark as the assumed mean (a) and calculating deviations (di) from it.

content.title: Assumed Mean Formula
content.text: Mean (x̄) = a + Σ(fi × di) / Σfi, where a is the assumed mean and di = xi − a (deviation from assumed mean).
The assumed mean is typically chosen as the class mark of a class near the middle of the distribution. This choice reduces the magnitude of deviations and simplifies calculations.

content.title: Example 2: Assumed Mean Method
content.problem: Using the data from Example 1, find the mean using assumed mean method with a = 25.

content.text: The step deviation method further simplifies calculations when all classes have equal width. Instead of deviations, we use step deviations (ui), where ui = di / h, and h is the class width.

content.title: Step Deviation Formula
content.text: Mean (x̄) = a + [Σ(fi × ui) / Σfi] × h, where ui = (xi − a) / h and h is the class width.
This method is the most efficient for large datasets with uniform class widths, as it minimizes arithmetic operations and reduces computational errors.

content.title: Example 3: Step Deviation Method
content.problem: Using Example 1 data with a = 25 and h = 10.

content.text: The mode is the value that appears most frequently in a dataset. For grouped data, we first identify the modal class—the class interval with the highest frequency. The actual mode is then estimated using a formula that accounts for the frequencies of adjacent classes.

content.title: Modal Class Definition
content.text: The modal class is the class interval with the maximum frequency. In a frequency distribution, it represents where the data is concentrated.

content.title: Mode Formula
content.text: Mode = l + [(f1 − f0) / (2f1 − f0 − f2)] × h, where: l = lower limit of modal class, f1 = frequency of modal class, f0 = frequency of class preceding modal class, f2 = frequency of class succeeding modal class, h = class width.
This formula is based on the assumption that the mode lies within the modal class and accounts for the frequencies of adjacent classes to refine the estimate.

content.title: Example 4: Finding Mode
content.problem: In a frequency distribution, the modal class is 20-30 with frequency 15. The preceding class has frequency 8, and the succeeding class has frequency 10. Find the mode.

content.text: The cumulative frequency of a class is the sum of frequencies of all classes up to and including that class. Cumulative frequency helps locate the median and is essential for constructing ogives (cumulative frequency curves).

content.title: Cumulative Frequency
content.text: CF(i) = f1 + f2 + ... + fi. It represents the running total of frequencies as we move through classes.
The median class is the class interval where the cumulative frequency first exceeds n/2 (where n is the total frequency). This class contains the median value.

content.title: Median Class Identification
content.text: The median class is where CF ≥ n/2 for the first time. The position of the median is n/2.

content.title: Median Formula
content.text: Median = l + [(n/2 − CF) / f] × h, where: l = lower limit of median class, n = total frequency, CF = cumulative frequency of class before median class, f = frequency of median class, h = class width.

content.title: Example 6: Finding Median
content.problem: In a frequency distribution with total frequency 100, the median class is 30-40 with frequency 20. The cumulative frequency before this class is 40. Find the median.

topics.topic_name: Relationship Between Mean, Median, and Mode

content.title: Empirical Relationship
content.text: There exists an empirical relationship between the three measures of central tendency: Mode = 3 × Median − 2 × Mean, or equivalently, Median = Mode + (1/3) × (Mean − Mode).
This relationship holds approximately for most distributions and provides a useful check on calculations. The three measures are equal for a perfectly symmetrical distribution.

An ogive is a smooth curve drawn by plotting cumulative frequencies against class boundaries. There are two types: less than ogive and greater than ogive. Ogives are useful for finding medians and percentiles graphically.

content.title: Less Than Ogive
content.text: Plot points (upper class limit, cumulative frequency). The curve rises from left to right and is used to find values below a certain point.

content.title: Greater Than Ogive
content.text: Plot points (lower class limit, cumulative frequency from the right). The curve falls from left to right and is used to find values above a certain point.

The median can be found graphically from a less than ogive by locating n/2 on the cumulative frequency axis, drawing a horizontal line to meet the curve, then dropping a perpendicular to the x-axis. The value where the perpendicular meets the x-axis is the median.

content.title: Example 7: Median from Ogive
content.problem: An ogive shows that at the point (35, 50), we are at the median of a distribution with total frequency 100. Interpret this.
content.solution: The cumulative frequency of 50 corresponds to 35 on the data axis. This means exactly half of the 100 observations have values ≤ 35. Therefore, the median is 35.

glossary.definition: Data organized into class intervals with their corresponding frequencies.
A range of values used to group data; defined by lower and upper class limits.
The average of the lower and upper class limits; represents all observations in the class.
The difference between the upper and lower limits of a class interval.
The number of observations falling in a particular class interval.
The sum of frequencies of a class and all classes preceding it.
The average value of all observations; calculated as Σ(fi × xi) / Σfi for grouped data.
The middle value when data is arranged in order; for grouped data, found using the median formula.
The value that appears most frequently; for grouped data, the midpoint of the modal class is used as an approximation.
The class interval with the highest frequency.
An arbitrary class mark chosen as a reference point to simplify calculations in the assumed mean method.
The difference between a class mark and the assumed mean: di = xi − a.
The deviation divided by class width: ui = (xi − a) / h.
A cumulative frequency curve used to visualize cumulative frequencies and find medians graphically.
A curve where points (upper class limit, cumulative frequency) are plotted and connected.
A curve where points (lower class limit, cumulative frequency from right) are plotted and connected.
The class interval where the cumulative frequency first exceeds or equals n/2.
Values (mean, median, mode) that represent the typical or central value of a dataset.

summary_points: Grouped data analysis extends the concepts of mean, median, and mode from ungrouped data. The mean for grouped data is calculated using three methods: (1) Direct Method: x̄ = Σ(fi × xi) / Σfi, (2) Assumed Mean Method: x̄ = a + Σ(fi × di) / Σfi, (3) Step Deviation Method: x̄ = a + [Σ(fi × ui) / Σfi] × h. The assumed mean method and step deviation method reduce computational complexity for large values.

The mode of grouped data is found using the formula: Mode = l + [(f1 − f0) / (2f1 − f0 − f2)] × h. The modal class is identified as the class with the highest frequency. The median for grouped data is calculated using: Median = l + [(n/2 − CF) / f] × h, where the median class is located by finding where cumulative frequency first exceeds n/2.

Cumulative frequency is the running total of frequencies up to each class. It is essential for finding medians and constructing ogives. An ogive is a cumulative frequency curve; the less than ogive plots (upper limit, CF) while the greater than ogive plots (lower limit, CF from right). Medians and other percentiles can be read graphically from ogives.

An empirical relationship exists among central tendencies: Mode = 3 × Median − 2 × Mean, or equivalently, Mode ≈ 3 Median − 2 Mean. This relationship holds approximately for most distributions and provides validation for calculations. For symmetrical distributions, all three measures are approximately equal.

When working with grouped data, ensure class intervals are continuous and of equal width (for step deviation method). The choice of assumed mean significantly affects ease of calculation but not the final result. Different methods are equivalent; choose based on the magnitude of class marks and uniformity of class widths for computational efficiency.
